Nanotoxicity Unveiled: Evaluating Exposure Risks and Assessing the Impact of Nanoparticles on Human Health
==========================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Rohit Kumar 1, Akhilesh Kumar 1, Sweety Bhardwaj 2, Mohini Sikarwar 2, Sonam Sriwastaw 3, Gaurav Sharma 4, Madhu Gupta 5

Show more

Outline

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jtemin.2025.100252](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jtemin.2025.100252 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S2773050625000436&orderBeanReset=true)

Under a Creative Commons [license](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/)

Open access

Highlights
----------

*   •This review explores the dual-edged nature of nanotechnology, showcasing its significant health risks to human health and the environment. 
*   •It emphasizes how nanoparticles can induce reactive oxygen species, triggering oxidative stress, inflammation, and disturbances in cellular antioxidant defenses. 
*   •Fundamental physicochemical properties like size, shape, and surface charge are discussed for their critical role in determining nanoparticle toxicity. 
*   •The paper also highlights nanoparticles’ ability to cross physiological barriers, potentially accumulating in various organs and associated toxicities. 
*   •This paper discusses various methods for assessing toxicity and examines the current landscape of regulatory efforts surrounding nanotoxicity. 

Abstract
--------

### Background

Nanomaterials have been widely used across medical and health sciences due to their unique physicochemical characteristics, versatile functionalisation, and remarkable tissue penetration abilities. As nanotechnology continues to evolve, concerns regarding the potential toxicological effects of these materials are growing. Despite their promising biomedical applications, comprehensive safety data remains limited.

### Purpose

This review details the physicochemical properties of the nanoparticles contributing to the development of potentially adverse effects on human health and the environment. It explores the cellular and molecular mechanisms through which nanoparticles induce toxicity. It assesses current nanotoxicity evaluation strategies, including _in vitro, in vivo,_ and _in silico_ models, along with supporting methodologies. The review also addresses the regulatory landscape of nanotoxicology, outlining the challenges in developing standardised protocols to ensure the safe and effective use of nanomaterials in the health sector.

### Key Observations

Factors such as particle size, dosage regimen, surface chemistry, and immunogenic potential of nanomaterials play a pivotal role in nanotoxicity. Nanoparticles may accumulate in diverse tissues, leading to oxidative stress, inflammation, and cellular and mitochondrial DNA damage. While regulatory agencies like the FDA, EMA, and CDE have issued guidelines for the safer use of nanomaterials, a globally harmonised framework is still absent.

### Conclusions

A deep understanding of nanotoxicity is crucial for the safe and sustainable development of nanomaterials. Future efforts should incorporate artificial intelligence and machine learning to predict, assess, and mitigate nanotoxicity by analysing complex data, identifying patterns, and refining nanoparticle design.

Nanomedicines

Toxicity of nanomaterials

Biomedical

Nanosafety

1. Introduction
---------------

Nanotechnology is a multidisciplinary field that enables manipulating materials at the nanometer scale (10⁻⁹ m), creating novel products with enhanced properties. The ability to tailor attributes such as conductivity, durability, reactivity, lifespan, and surface area at the nanoscale has led to wide-ranging applications across chemistry, medicine, and cosmetics [[[1]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773050625000436#bib0001), [[2]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773050625000436#bib0002), [[3]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773050625000436#bib0003), [[4]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773050625000436#bib0004)]. Among these, nanoparticles (NPs) have emerged as particularly valuable due to their unique dimensions, malleability, and versatility, making them ideal for various industrial and biomedical purposes [[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773050625000436#bib0005)]. Several engineered nanoparticles, such as polymeric NPs, micelles, liposomes, and nanocrystals, have received approval from the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) as chemotherapeutic agents [[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773050625000436#bib0006),[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773050625000436#bib0007)]. These nanocarriers treat numerous diseases, such as cancer [[8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773050625000436#bib0008)], rheumatoid arthritis, Crohn's disease, hepatitis B and C, renal disorders, and diabetes. Moreover, the strategic use of encapsulated immunosuppressants to enhance targeted delivery in transplant patients marks a significant milestone in pharmaceutical development [[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773050625000436#bib0009),[10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773050625000436#bib0010)]. Despite their promising potential, the rapid advancement of nanotechnology has outpaced our understanding of nanomaterial's safety and long-term effects. The properties that make NPs effective are their small size and high reactivity, which raises concerns about potential toxicity to humans and the environment [[11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773050625000436#bib0011)]. Nanotoxicology is a field that studies the adverse effects of nanomaterials and plays a critical role in identifying how NPs interact with cellular systems. NPs exhibit unique physicochemical properties such as composition, morphology, charge, and size, influencing their cellular interactions and toxicity profiles [[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773050625000436#bib0012)]. NPs are internalised through nano-specific pathways upon exposure, initiating a cascade of cellular events. These particles can bind to specific membrane receptors and activate intracellular signalling pathways through NADPH oxidase, calcium flux, and reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation. The subsequent signal transduction activates transcription factors like NF-κB and AP-1, which modulate gene expression linked to inflammatory responses, genotoxicity, and fibrosis [[13](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773050625000436#bib0013)]. NPs can undergo intracellular translocation, potentially disrupting organelle function ([Figure 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773050625000436#fig0001)). The cellular outcomes include apoptosis, necrosis, or aberrant proliferation, depending on the nature and extent of NP exposure. Understanding these mechanisms is crucial for evaluating NP safety and designing safer nanomaterials for biomedical applications through _in vitro, in vivo_, and _in silico_ models [[14](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773050625000436#bib0014),[15](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773050625000436#bib0015)]. _In vitro_ models are available for nanotoxicity assessment, including immortalised cell lines, primary cell cultures, and tissue-derived models.

![Image 12: Figure 1](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2773050625000436-gr1.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (605KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2773050625000436-gr1_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (605KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2773050625000436-gr1.jpg "Download full-size image")

Figure 1. Factors responsible for NPs cytotoxicity and corresponding cellular responses (Created with Biorender).

In contrast, cell lines are widely used because of their consistency, ease of cultivation, and cost-effectiveness. However, as they are typically derived from cancer cells or artificially immortalised, they may not accurately reflect normal cellular responses due to altered signalling pathways. Nanomaterials can induce toxicity through various mechanisms, including oxidative stress, inflammation, genotoxicity, and different forms of cell death, such as apoptosis, autophagy, and necrosis. Several assays evaluate these endpoints, which are broadly categorised into cytotoxicity assessments, oxidative stress, pro-inflammatory responses, and genotoxic effects. Cell viability and proliferation are commonly measured using colourimetric or fluorometric assays such as MTT, XTT, WST-1, Alamar Blue, or neutral red uptake, along with membrane integrity assays like LDH release or trypan blue exclusion. Further, assays like TUNEL, caspase activation, and Annexin V/PI staining help determine the specific modes of cell death triggered by nanomaterials. Peters _et al._ (2020) quantified silicon dioxide (SiO₂) and titanium dioxide (TiO₂) particles in postmortem human tissues, including the liver, spleen, kidney, jejunum, and ileum through electron microscopy [[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773050625000436#bib0016)]. They employed validated digestion methods and spICP-HRMS to detect total forms, revealing that SiO₂ comprised about 10% of total silicon, while TiO₂ accounted for roughly 80% of total titanium. The particle sizes varied, with a notable proportion of TiO₂ particles measuring below 100 nm. These results highlight the systemic uptake and accumulation of engineered nanoparticles in human tissues, likely stemming from environmental and dietary exposure [[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773050625000436#bib0016)].

_In vivo_ toxicity assessments are typically conducted using animal models such as mice and rats to evaluate the biological effects of nanoparticles. Standard evaluation methods include biodistribution, clearance, haematological analysis, serum biochemistry, and histopathological examination. Biodistribution studies track the accumulation and localisation of nanoparticles within tissues and organs, often using radiolabeling techniques in both live and sacrificed animals [[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773050625000436#bib0017)]. Clearance analysis involves monitoring nanoparticle excretion and metabolic fate at various time intervals post-exposure to understand their persistence and elimination routes. Changes in serum biochemical parameters and blood cell profiles can also provide valuable indicators of systemic toxicity. Histopathological analysis is a critical approach to assessing tissue-level damage caused by nanoparticle exposure. These techniques involve microscopic examination of tissues such as the lungs, liver, kidneys, brain, spleen, heart, and eyes to detect morphological changes or inflammatory responses [[18](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773050625000436#bib0018)]. Recent advancements in toxicity evaluation have introduced technologies like micro-electrochemistry and microfluidics. These offer high sensitivity and precision in monitoring nanoparticle-induced biological changes, enhancing our ability to predict and mitigate potential health risks. _Drosophila melanogaster_ and zebrafish (_Danio rerio_) are valuable models for assessing the _in vivo_ toxicity of nanomaterials due to their genetic tractability, short life cycles, and cost-effectiveness. Zebrafish share approximately 70% of their genes with humans, making them a potent model for analysis of developmental and toxicological studies [[19](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773050625000436#bib0019)]. They are a crucial bridge between _in vitro_ experiments and mammalian _in vivo_ models. Similarly, _Drosophila_ offers powerful genetic tools and conserved biological pathways, enabling high-throughput screening and mechanistic insights into nanomaterial-induced toxicity. These models provide complementary platforms for evaluating nanomaterial safety and biological interactions. Chauhan _et al._ (2024) investigated the toxicological effects of a ZnFe₂O₄@poly(_t_ BGE-_alt_-PA) nanocomposite using _Drosophila melanogaster_ as a model organism [[20](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773050625000436#bib0020)]. Their research assessed multiple toxicity endpoints, including neurological, developmental, genotoxic, and morphological parameters. Significant findings included impaired crawling behaviour in third-instar larvae and altered climbing ability in adult flies, indicating potential neurotoxicity. Fluorescent staining with DAPI and DCFH-DA further revealed abnormalities in the larval gut, while morphological deformities were shown in the wings and eyes of exposed flies [[20](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773050625000436#bib0020)]. These results collectively highlight the nanocomposite's toxic impact on fruit flies, emphasizing the need to evaluate its biological safety carefully.

Numerous _in silico_ models and computational methods have been developed to evaluate and predict the potential toxicity of chemical substances, offering valuable insights into their risks to human health and the environment. The growing recognition of the limitations of traditional toxicity testing, particularly the ethical and financial concerns associated with animal studies [[21](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773050625000436#bib0021)], has underscored the importance of integrating _in silico_ approaches into risk assessment frameworks [[22](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773050625000436#bib0022)]. These models facilitate the early identification of hazardous substances, enabling proactive risk management and minimizing late-stage failures in drug development due to unforeseen toxic effects. _In silico_ toxicology employs many tools, including curated databases, molecular descriptor generators, simulation platforms, and predictive modelling techniques. These methods offer cost-effective and high-throughput alternatives to _in vivo_ testing and enhance the ability to screen large chemical libraries rapidly [[22](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773050625000436#bib0022)]. By enabling early toxicity prediction, these approaches support safer drug design and regulatory decision-making while reducing reliance on animal testing. Due to limited knowledge regarding the behaviour of NPs in biological systems, regulatory agencies are increasingly prioritizing assessing their safety and toxicological profiles [[23](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773050625000436#bib0023),[24](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773050625000436#bib0024)]. While the benefits of nanotechnology are undeniable, ensuring its safe and responsible application requires a comprehensive evaluation of its associated hazards.

This review aims to expand the current understanding of nanomaterial toxicity by exploring the interplay between their physicochemical characteristics and biological effects. In addition to highlighting toxicological mechanisms, this review addresses conceptual gaps in the field and serves as a foundational resource for students and researchers. It provides insights into the impact of nanomaterials on various biological systems, offering an updated perspective that contributes to the growing body of nanotoxicological research.

### 1.1. Nanotoxicity- Human Health and Environmental Impacts

Despite the significant advantages of nanoparticles, critical concerns regarding their potential effects on human health and the environment remain unresolved. It is imperative to address these issues comprehensively before the widespread production and application of nanomaterials. Engineered nanoparticles, increasingly used in the industry and consumer field, are emerging contaminants with potential ecological and toxicological effects on populations, communities, and ecosystems. The field of nanotoxicology analyzes the threats that nanomaterials pose to people and the environment. Engineered nanomaterial toxicity is often determined by many factors, including (1) their plasmonic capabilities, (2) coating assessment, (3) particle shape and size, (4) surface charge, and (5) their phase stability [[25](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773050625000436#bib0025),[26](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773050625000436#bib0026)]. The use of engineered nanomaterials (ENMs) in human life is continuously increasing, which can be an alarming concern related to their safety. Such NMs may cause acute, chronic, and systemic toxicities in humans after interacting with the biological microenvironment. As a result, it can lead to various life-threatening diseases and harm the environment

Further analysing the toxicity of such ENMs before their use is essential to reduce their possible adverse effects. Nevertheless, studies conducted in the 20th century proved that materials with micrometre-scale dimensions demonstrated less inherent hazardous characteristics, whereas those at nanoscale dimensions exhibited plausible toxicity [[27](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773050625000436#bib0027),[28](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773050625000436#bib0028)]. A comprehensive understanding of nanomaterial toxicity requires an in-depth analysis of their physicochemical characteristics. Key factors such as particle size, shape, composition, concentration, structural dimensions, and stability play a crucial role. Additionally, surface-related attributes, including morphology, roughness, surface energy, charge, and the presence of functional groups, significantly influence nanoparticle behaviour [[29](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773050625000436#bib0029)]. These properties collectively determine how nanoparticles interact with biological systems, including their cellular uptake, internal distribution, and interactions with intracellular components. Such interactions can trigger a cascade of biological responses, including the release of reactive ions from the nanoparticle surface, generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), lipid peroxidation, protein denaturation, inflammation, mitochondrial dysfunction, disruption of endothelial function, impaired phagocytosis, and disturbances in cell cycle regulation [[29](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773050625000436#bib0029),[30](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773050625000436#bib0030)]. Among cellular organelles, mitochondria are especially vulnerable to damage from nanomaterials. They play a central role in mediating nanotoxic effects. Research has demonstrated that exposure to nanomaterials can lead to significant changes in mitochondrial structure, disrupt membrane potential, trigger the opening of the mitochondrial permeability transition pore (MPTP), and impair respiratory function. Mitochondria are often involved in cell death pathways activated by nanomaterial exposure as the core regulators of intrinsic apoptosis [[31](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773050625000436#bib0031),[32](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773050625000436#bib0032)]. Environmental stressors, such as increased reactive oxygen species (ROS), can prompt cells to adjust mitochondrial behaviour by modulating mitochondrial dynamics and initiating mitophagy processes essential for maintaining mitochondrial integrity and overall cellular homeostasis. Notably, nanomaterials can influence these dynamics: at lower concentrations, they promote mitochondrial fusion, while at higher concentrations, they are associated with excessive fragmentation. Fusion may serve as a cellular defence mechanism to sustain energy production and limit damage, whereas controlled fission can isolate damaged regions of mitochondria, preserving healthy segments [[31](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773050625000436#bib0031),[32](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773050625000436#bib0032)].

The environmental toxicity of nanomaterials stems from the many properties that make them harmful to living cells. These characteristics can adversely impact microorganisms, plants, soil and water quality, air purity, agricultural productivity, pollution levels, and aquatic ecosystems. To better understand the physical and chemical interactions involved, it is essential to recognise that when nanomaterials come into contact with water, air, or soil, a surface coating known as "corona" forms around them. This corona influences how the nanomaterials interact with cellular components. NPs can have varying impacts on plants, ranging from neutral to beneficial or harmful, depending on their type, size, concentration, duration of exposure, application method, plant species involved, and surrounding environmental conditions. Due to their small size, high surface area, and catalytic properties, NPs can penetrate plant cells through the cell membrane and potentially disrupt intracellular functions by interacting with key biomolecules [[33](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773050625000436#bib0033)]. The phytotoxic effects of NPs often stem from their ability to interfere with nutrient uptake and accumulate within plant tissues, which can impair essential cellular activities. These disruptions may lead to reduced seedling numbers, inhibited root and shoot growth, delayed flowering, and restricted seed germination—all contributing to diminished crop yield. Once internalised, NPs can alter cellular and molecular structures, compromise the membrane integrity, and interfere with the plant's defence systems. Ke _et al._ (2020) showed that silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) significantly impaired floral development and reduced the reproductive efficiency in Arabidopsis thal

URL: https://ai2-s2-public.s3.amazonaws.com/figures/2017-08-08/8e2566563ab280164c0134eedff8553c7eb2136b/3-Figure1-1.png
DESCRIPTION: A flowchart illustrating the process of predicting nanoparticle cytotoxicity in nanotoxicology assessment, including literature reports, experimental conditions, mammalian cells images, and a nano QSTR-perturbation model using StatSoft Statistica software.

IMAGE 3:
URL: https://medcraveonline.com/JNMR/images/JNMR-06-00149-g001.png
DESCRIPTION: The diagram illustrates various nanotoxicological mechanisms of cytotoxicity, including interactions with lysosomes, vesicles, the nucleus, mitochondria, the Golgi apparatus, and cell membranes, leading to cellular damage such as DNA damage, lipid peroxidation, protein misfolding, and membrane disruption.

IMAGE 4:
URL: https://pubs.rsc.org/en/Image/Get?imageInfo.ImageType=GA&imageInfo.ImageIdentifier.ManuscriptID=C9NR08714A&imageInfo.ImageIdentifier.Year=2020
DESCRIPTION: The illustration depicts the difference between non-polymerized and polymerized micelles, highlighting how polymerization enhances membrane internalization, reduces cytotoxicity, and prevents inflammation and genotoxicity in nanotoxicology assessments.

URL: https://www.mdpi.com/ijms/ijms-22-10952/article_deploy/html/images/ijms-22-10952-g001.png
DESCRIPTION: None

enriched_points.expanded_content: Cytotoxicity findings in vitro provide crucial insights into nanoparticle safety, indicating potential toxic effects before progressing to in vivo studies, thereby guiding regulatory and clinical decisions.

In vitro studies often lack systemic complexity; they cannot fully replicate the physiological environment, leading to potential misinterpretations regarding the real biological impact of nanomaterials.

While in vitro results offer preliminary data, they must correlate with in vivo findings to validate toxicity assessments, ensuring a comprehensive understanding of nanoparticle behavior in living organisms.
Understanding in vitro results is essential for risk assessment, informing safety evaluations and regulatory frameworks prior to the clinical application of nanomaterials in medicine and industry.
